claimed_contribution: Operationalizes spatial prediction (kriging) for marketing multimarket panel data and estimates it in a Bayesian MCMC framework.
Develops a market similarity covariance model that combines geographic distance with retailer-territory structure and retailer interaction, instead of using distance only.
Provides a way to improve local marketing-mix inference in markets with poor data by modeling temporal and spatial dependence and borrowing information from nearby/related markets.
context: The paper is set in U.S. grocery scanner-data markets for nationally distributed packaged goods, with empirical application to Mexican salsa (and also tortilla chips). It studies cross-market brand performance across 64 IRI markets while leveraging the U.S. retail-chain network across designated market areas.
design_and_data: The authors combine weekly IRI INFOSCAN data (May 1994-April 1996) from about 3000 stores in 64 markets with TradeDimension retailer account ACV data, plus geographic coordinates and census demographics. They estimate a hierarchical log-log demand and price system with spatially varying demand constants and price elasticities, AR(1) temporal dynamics, and spatial covariance components (distance-based, retailer-based, and independent). Estimation uses Bayesian MCMC (40,000 draws, burn-in and thinning), and prediction performance is tested with multiple spatial holdout designs (random removals, circles, East-West bands, North-South bands) against naive benchmarks.
main_findings: U.S. retailer territories are shingled rather than isolated, and retailer interaction is estimated to be locally imitative.
Retailer structure explains a substantial share of cross-market variation in brand performance, even after controlling for distance-based spatial effects.

Accounting for spatial structure yields more efficient and more plausible price elasticity estimates and clearly outperforms naive predictors in holdout tests, with especially strong gains when holdout samples are large.
one_line_summary: The paper develops a Bayesian spatiotemporal kriging approach that uses both retailer overlap and geographic distance to predict brand performance in unsampled or weakly sampled U.S. markets and improves inference on price elasticities.
relation_to_prior_work: introduces spatial prediction to marketing multimarket analysis, an area the authors argue had largely ignored explicit cross-sectional dependence. It extends distance-only geostatistical prediction by adding retailer-based covariance tied to retail territories and competitive interaction, and it integrates measurement of latent market effects with prediction.
research_gap: The paper states that many relevant U.S. markets are unsampled in syndicated data, creating a missing-data problem for local decision making. It also argues that marketing models had rarely addressed spatial cross-market dependence, so formal spatial prediction methods were largely absent in this domain.
research_question: How can multimarket scanner data be used to predict brand performance in markets with no data or poor data? Specifically, can a spatiotemporal model that combines geographic proximity and shared retailer structure produce better out-of-sample predictions and more credible local price elasticity estimates than common naive or nonspatial approaches?
